CARVEYANCE

Carveyance · Engineering FEATURE / 19 MIN READ

One-Wheel
Drive

Every car has a device that lets it turn a corner without dragging a tyre. The same device will strand you in two inches of mud with the engine howling and one wheel spinning uselessly in the air. This is the hundred-year argument about how to fix that — and why the winning answer turned out to be the brakes.

By Carveyance Editorial · Differentials, limited-slip, lockers and traction control

Invented by
A watchmaker
Onésiphore Pecqueur, Paris, 1827 — for a steam wagon
Torque split, open diff
50 / 50
Always. Even when one wheel is in the air. Especially then
First electronic traction control
Buick, 1971
MaxTrac, on the Riviera. Dropped after about two years
The Setup

The Silver Bullet
and the Skylark

I was sixteen, maybe seventeen, and I drove a silver Volvo 240 DL wagon that I had christened the Silver Bullet on the grounds that it was, in every measurable respect, not a bullet.

My mother and I were moving furniture from her friend Barbara's house back to ours. Coming out of Barbara's neighbourhood I missed the turn, spotted a grassy verge big enough to swing the wagon around in, and took it. It had rained lightly the night before. Not a storm — just enough to turn the top inch of that grass into mud.

The Bullet sank, and then it did the thing.

Right rear tyre spinning merrily, flinging mud, engine revving, speedometer indicating that we were making excellent progress. Left rear tyre: motionless. Sitting there on perfectly good ground doing absolutely nothing, like a man watching his colleague struggle with a heavy box.

My mother — who is not an engineer and has never claimed to be — looked at this and asked the single best question anyone has ever asked me about cars:

"How do they make a car with one-wheel drive in a place where it snows?"

I had no answer. We called my dad, who arrived in his Grand Cherokee and pulled us out, which did nothing to improve my standing in the matter.

And then, a few years later, a courtroom

You know the scene. Two kids from New York are on trial in Alabama for a murder at a convenience store they didn't commit, and the entire case comes down to a photograph of tyre marks in the dirt outside. Marisa Tomei, as Mona Lisa Vito, is on the stand.

Her testimony is that the marks could not have come from the defendants' 1964 Buick Skylark, because the car that made them had Positraction — and the Skylark didn't offer it. Vinny, playing dumb for the jury, asks her what Positraction is.

And her answer is, word for word:

"It's a limited slip differential which distributes power equally to both the right and left tires. The '64 Skylark had a regular differential, which, anyone who's been stuck in the mud in Alabama knows, you step on the gas, one tire spins, the other tire does nothing."

One tyre spins. The other tyre does nothing.

That is not a similar problem to my mother's question. That is the same sentence, delivered by a fictional hair salon employee in a Hollywood courtroom in 1992, about a mud patch in Alabama, and it describes a silver Volvo wagon stuck on a verge in a suburb with a teenager at the wheel.

My mother was not confused. She had independently diagnosed, from the passenger seat, the central design compromise of the automobile — the one component that every car has, that nobody thinks about, and that has generated a century of increasingly clever engineering attempts to work around its one enormous flaw.

She just called it one-wheel drive. Which, honestly, is the better name.

I · Why the Thing Exists

The Problem Before
the Problem

Before we convict the differential, it deserves a hearing — because it was invented to solve a real problem, and it solves it perfectly.

Take a corner. Any corner. Your outside wheels travel a longer arc than your inside wheels, which means that over the course of that turn the outside wheel must physically rotate more times than the inside one. Not slightly more. Measurably more.

Now bolt both driven wheels to one solid shaft, which is the obvious way to build a car. Both wheels are now forced to rotate at identical speeds. But the corner demands they rotate at different speeds. Something has to give, and what gives is the tyres — one of them has to skip, scrub or drag its way around the bend, chirping and shredding itself and fighting the steering the whole way.

This was not a theoretical concern. It was ruining wheels, axles and road surfaces on horse-drawn wagons long before anyone bolted an engine to anything.

The watchmaker's answer

The fix came from a man who spent his life thinking about gears that rotate at different rates in the same housing: Onésiphore Pecqueur, a French watchmaker working at the Conservatoire des Arts et Métiers in Paris. In 1827 he built the mechanical differential — for a steam wagon, decades before the internal combustion engine existed.

He is sometimes called the world's first automotive engineer, and the lineage is worth sitting with. The thing keeping your rear tyres from scrubbing on a motorway off-ramp tonight is a clockmaking idea. It came out of the same craft tradition as a watch complication, and it works on exactly the same principle: cogs sharing one input while turning at their own rates.

Older than it looks Differential gearing may be far older than Pecqueur. The Antikythera mechanism — the Greek astronomical calculator recovered from a shipwreck and dated to roughly 100 BC — is argued by some researchers to use a differential gear to compute the angle between the Sun and Moon. The Chinese south-pointing chariot, documented around 250 AD, may have used one too. Both attributions are debated. What isn't debated is that Pecqueur was the first to put one under a vehicle.

How it actually works

Power arrives from the engine down the driveshaft, hits a small gear called the pinion, which turns a much larger gear called the ring. The ring gear is bolted to a rotating cage — the differential carrier. Inside that cage sit the spider gears (also called pinion gears), and those mesh with the side gears, which are splined to your two axle shafts.

In a straight line, nothing inside the cage moves relative to anything else. The whole assembly rotates as one lump and both wheels turn at the same speed.

In a corner, the inside wheel is forced to slow down. The spider gears begin rotating on their own pins — and because of how they're meshed, every rotation they make slows one side gear by exactly the same amount it speeds up the other. The average stays constant. The corner is absorbed.

Animation 01

Cornering: the spider gears absorb the speed difference
Simplified schematic. Wheel speeds shown are illustrative, not to scale. This is the differential doing exactly the job it was designed to do.

That is a genuinely elegant piece of engineering, and for a hundred and ninety-eight years nobody has found a reason to replace it.

II · The Flaw

Why It Betrays You
in the Mud

Here is where nearly every explanation of this — including, gently, the one Mona Lisa Vito gives on the stand — gets the physics slightly wrong. The correct version is stranger and much more satisfying.

People say the open differential "sends the power to the wheel with the least traction." It doesn't. It never does anything so decisive.

An open differential always splits torque equally between the two wheels. Fifty-fifty. Always, unconditionally, in every situation, including the one where your Volvo is stuck in a wet lawn. That is not a failure mode. That is the mechanism working precisely as designed.

The problem is what "equally" means when one side has nothing to push against.

The arithmetic of getting stuck

A wheel can only transmit as much torque as its grip allows. Exceed that and it spins. So the tyre sitting on wet mud might be able to accept, say, 30 newton-metres before it breaks traction and starts turning uselessly.

Now apply the rule. Both wheels get equal torque. The muddy wheel caps out at 30. Therefore the wheel on firm ground also receives 30 — not because anything decided to punish it, but because equal means equal, and the weakest wheel sets the ceiling for both.

Sixty newton-metres total, in a car making four hundred. Nowhere near enough to move.

The open differential doesn't send power to the wheel with no grip. It sends the same power to both — and lets the worse wheel decide how much that is.

Meanwhile the speed side is unconstrained. The carrier averages the two wheel speeds, so if one wheel is held at zero by good grip, the other is free to spin at double the speed the driveshaft is turning. Which is why a stuck wheel doesn't just spin — it spins with an enthusiasm that sounds like something is about to break.

Animation 02

One wheel on mud: equal torque, catastrophic result
Torque values illustrative. The gripping wheel is not being starved of torque — it is receiving exactly as much as the slipping wheel can accept, which is almost none.

Everything that follows in this article — every mechanical device, every patent, every line of software written since 1971 — is an attempt to break that equal-split rule without giving up the cornering ability that made the differential necessary in the first place.

There are essentially five answers. They arrived roughly in order, they all still exist, and the newest one is quietly making the older ones obsolete.

III · The Fixes

Five Ways to Beat
the Fifty-Fifty

01

Limited-slip — the polite compromise

The first idea: leave the differential free to do its cornering job, but add internal resistance so that when one wheel starts spinning wildly relative to the other, something inside fights that difference and shoves torque back toward the slower wheel.

This is a limited-slip differential, and General Motors sold it as Positraction — which is the single most successful piece of drivetrain branding in history, given that a movie made it a household word thirty years after the fact.

The correction Mona Lisa Vito would want Positraction was Chevrolet's trade name. Pontiac called its version Safe-T-Track. Oldsmobile had Anti-Spin, Buick had Positive Traction, Ford sold Equa-Lock and later Traction-Lok, Chrysler had Sure-Grip. The film uses "Positraction" generically — as does basically everyone now — but the '63 Tempest's system would properly have been Safe-T-Track. Enthusiasts have also long argued the Tempest was a shaky suspect and a later Corvair would have fit her criteria better. None of it damages her actual point, which is that the Skylark's plain open diff could not have laid two equal marks.

There are four main ways to build one, and the differences matter:

Clutch-type. Stacks of friction plates preloaded by springs sit between the side gears and the case. Speed difference means the clutches slip, and slipping clutches generate resistance that transfers torque to the slower wheel. Cheap, effective, tuneable — and consumable. The plates wear, and they want specific friction-modified oil.

Cone-type. Same idea, conical friction surfaces instead of plates. Simple and durable.

Viscous. Interleaved plates in a sealed housing of thick silicone fluid. Spin one set fast relative to the other and shearing heats the fluid, which thickens it, which couples the two sides. Utterly passive, no wear surfaces, and slow to react — it needs the speed difference to already be happening before it does anything.

Torsen — the clever one. Short for torque sensing, and it is a genuinely beautiful piece of thinking. Vernon Gleasman at Gleason Works patented the core idea in 1951 after noticing something about worm gears: a worm can drive a worm wheel, but a worm wheel cannot back-drive the worm. Build a differential around that asymmetry and you get a device that behaves like an open diff under normal driving, then automatically binds and biases torque toward the gripping side the instant a difference appears — with no clutches, no fluid, no sensors and no wear.

How hard each device pushes back

Approximate torque bias ratio — how much more torque the gripping wheel can receive
Torque bias ratio expresses the maximum ratio of torque to the high-grip wheel versus the low-grip wheel. Ranges are indicative — actual figures vary widely by design, preload, condition and manufacturer. A locked differential has no meaningful ratio because the axle is mechanically one piece.

The catch with all of them, and it's the same catch: a limited-slip needs the losing wheel to have some grip to work against. Put a wheel on genuinely frictionless ice — or lift it clean off the ground, which happens constantly off-road — and even a Torsen can revert to behaving like an open differential. There's nothing to multiply.

Hold onto that, because it becomes important later.

02

The locker — the blunt instrument

If the differential's ability to let wheels turn at different speeds is what's ruining your day, then remove it. A locking differential mechanically ties both axle shafts into one solid unit. Same speed, no exceptions, and torque can now go wherever grip exists — up to 100% on one side if the other is airborne.

This is the most effective traction device ever built, and it is also, on pavement, actively dangerous. You have deliberately recreated the exact problem Pecqueur solved in 1827. The car will not turn properly, the tyres chirp and scrub, the driveline winds up, and in the wet a locked axle will happily rotate the car for you.

Which is why the interesting engineering is all in when it locks:

Automatic lockers (the Detroit Locker being the archetype) are locked by default and ratchet apart when cornering forces demand it. Enormously effective, and they announce themselves with bangs and clunks that alarm passengers.

Selectable lockers let the driver choose. Air-actuated units like ARB's, and factory electric lockers such as those in the Jeep Wrangler Rubicon, sit open for the drive to the trail and lock on a switch when you get there. This is the right answer for almost everyone.

Spools are permanently locked — a solid piece with no differential action at all. Drag racing and dedicated competition only.

The count matters too. One locker (rear) is the common truck setup. Two (front and rear) is serious off-road hardware — Rubicon, Power Wagon. Three, adding a locking centre differential, is Mercedes G-Class territory and means all four wheels are mechanically forced to the same speed regardless of what any of them are touching.

·

An aside: the hubs on the front of old trucks

If you've owned anything older with four-wheel drive, you remember getting out in the rain to twist a dial on each front wheel.

Manual locking hubs physically connect or disconnect the front wheels from the front axle shafts. Unlocked, the wheels spin free and the entire front driveline — shafts, diff, front driveshaft — sits still, saving fuel, wear and noise. It is a completely sound piece of engineering whose only flaw is that it requires a human being to stand in the weather.

The replacements came in stages: automatic hubs that engaged on torque or vacuum and were reliably unreliable; then centre-axle disconnect, which uncouples the axle inside the housing instead; and finally today's electronically actuated systems, where a button or a dial does what your cold hands used to.

The honest trade: manual hubs are more durable, more predictable and repairable with hand tools; electronic systems are vastly more convenient and can engage while moving. Overlanders still fit manual hubs on purpose. Almost nobody else misses them.

IV · The Software Answer

The Fix Nobody
Saw Coming

03

Traction control — beating the diff with the brakes

Every solution so far has been a mechanical device inside the axle housing, costing money, weight and complexity. Then somebody realised the car already had four devices capable of stopping a wheel from spinning, one at each corner, fitted as standard.

Go back to the arithmetic. The gripping wheel is limited to whatever torque the slipping wheel can accept. So raise the amount the slipping wheel can accept — not by giving it grip, which you can't, but by applying its brake.

A braked wheel resists rotation. That resistance is functionally indistinguishable, to the differential, from traction. Feed the spinning wheel 200 Nm of brake and the differential dutifully sends 200 Nm to the other side as well — because it splits everything equally, which for the first time is working in your favour.

The differential's stupidest quality — that it insists on equal torque no matter what — turns into its most useful one, the moment you can lie to it with a brake caliper.

This is brake-based traction control, and it is a virtual locking differential made of software and existing hardware. No extra gears. No fluid. Nothing to wear beyond brake pads.

The Buick joke

Now for the detail that makes this whole article click into place.

The first production electronic traction control system was MaxTrac, launched in 1971. It used a transmission speed sensor, a front wheel speed sensor, a controller and an on-off switch: compare front and rear speeds, detect slip, cut ignition to reduce power. It appeared on full-size cars including the Riviera, Electra and LeSabre.

It was built by Buick.

The same division whose 1964 Skylark, seven years earlier, shipped with a plain open differential and no Positraction option — the very fact that put two innocent men in an Alabama courtroom. Buick spent the sixties selling cars that couldn't put power down and then invented the electronic fix for it.

The market had no idea what to do with it. Buyers didn't understand it, dealers couldn't sell it, and it was dropped after about two years. Cadillac tried again with a Traction Monitoring System on the 1979 Eldorado. The technology then essentially waited for the rest of the car to catch up — specifically, for anti-lock brakes to become common, because ABS brought exactly the two things traction control needed: a wheel speed sensor at every corner, and a hydraulic unit that could apply an individual brake without the driver's foot.

Once that hardware was standard, traction control became nearly free. Bosch systems arrived on Mercedes in the mid-eighties. And in 1993 Land Rover put brake-based traction control on the Discovery — using it to do off-road what a mechanical locker did, without the locker.

The century of workarounds

Mechanical solutions gave way to electronic ones
1827Pecqueur's differential. A watchmaker solves cornering, and creates the problem this entire article is about.
1930s–50sLimited-slip goes mainstream. Clutch and cone-type units reach production; GM markets Positraction and its sibling names across divisions.
1951Gleasman patents the Torsen principle — worm gears that bias torque mechanically, with no clutches to wear out.
1971Buick MaxTrac. First production electronic traction control. Cuts ignition on detected slip. Dropped within about two years.
1979Cadillac Traction Monitoring System on the Eldorado — the idea refuses to die.
Mid-1980sABS becomes common, and with it the wheel-speed sensors and hydraulic modulators that make real traction control cheap. Bosch systems reach Mercedes.
1993Land Rover Discovery uses brake-based traction control off-road — software doing a locker's job.
2000sStability control becomes mandatory in major markets, putting the hardware for electronic traction management in essentially every new car.
2010s–nowTorque vectoring and electric drive. The car stops equalising torque and starts deliberately unbalancing it — then, with a motor per wheel, deletes the differential altogether.
Compiled from Britannica and Gleason/Torsen patent records, HandWiki and contemporary press on GM MaxTrac and Cadillac TMS, and manufacturer histories. Dates mark introduction, not universal adoption.

The varieties, and what the button actually does

"Traction control" now covers several distinct interventions, usually working together:

Brake-based (the virtual locker). Applies an individual wheel's brake to create artificial resistance. Adds traction. This is the one doing the clever work.

Engine-based (torque reduction). Cuts throttle, retards ignition timing, drops cylinders or reduces boost. Doesn't add traction — reduces demand. Safe, effective, and the reason your car sometimes feels like it has gone limp mid-corner.

Stability control is the bigger sibling and a different job. Traction control stops wheels spinning under power. Stability control adds a steering angle sensor and a yaw sensor, compares where you're pointing with where you're going, and brakes individual wheels to rotate the car back into line. It is the single most effective safety system fitted to cars since the seatbelt.

Off-road and terrain modes retune the same hardware. On tarmac, wheelspin is failure and should be shut down immediately. In deep sand, wheelspin is how you make progress and shutting it down strands you. That's the entire difference between "Sand" and "Snow" on the dial.

What the off button really means Pressing "traction control off" in most cars raises the intervention threshold rather than genuinely disabling everything, and stability control usually stays partly live until you hold the button or dig into a menu. There are three legitimate reasons to reduce it: rocking a stuck vehicle out of snow or mud, where you need sustained wheelspin; deliberate low-grip driving on a closed course; and certain deep sand and dune conditions. On a public road in poor weather, the system is better at this than you are, and that isn't an insult — it can brake one wheel in a few milliseconds and you cannot.
V · Past Equality

Then Engineers Got
Greedy

04

Torque vectoring — deliberately unfair

Everything so far has been about restoring balance: stop that wheel spinning, get torque back to the wheel that can use it. Torque vectoring asks a more ambitious question. Why stop at equal?

In a corner, the outside wheel has more load on it, more grip available, and is travelling further. Send it more torque than the inside wheel — not equal, deliberately more — and the resulting thrust imbalance pushes the car's nose into the turn. You are steering with the drivetrain.

Two ways to do it. Brake-based vectoring nips the inside wheel to create the imbalance — cheap, uses existing hardware, and scrubs off a little speed doing it. True mechanical vectoring uses clutch packs or a step-up gearset to actively overdrive the outer wheel, adding torque instead of subtracting it. Honda's SH-AWD, Mitsubishi's AYC on the Evo, and the systems in cars like the Focus RS and modern M and AMG products work this way. Far better, far more expensive.

05

Electric motors — deleting the problem

And then the answer that makes the previous four look quaint.

If each wheel — or each axle — has its own electric motor, there is no shared torque to divide. No spider gears, no side gears, no equal-split rule, nothing to lock, nothing to trick with a brake caliper. Torque at each wheel becomes an independent software variable, adjustable thousands of times per second.

Two hundred years of increasingly clever mechanical devices existed to work around one consequence of one shaft feeding two wheels. Remove the shared shaft and the entire problem, along with its entire solution industry, simply evaporates.

Which is a strange thing to celebrate. The Torsen is a lovely object. So is a Detroit Locker, in its violent way. They are the kind of engineering that solves a hard problem with shapes and materials rather than instructions — and they're being replaced by a control algorithm you will never see, hear or need to service.

The best solution to the differential turned out to be not having one.

The centre differential, briefly

Everything above concerns two wheels on one axle. Drive all four and you have the identical problem one level up — front and rear axles also need to turn at different speeds in a corner, or the driveline binds. So you fit a centre differential, and it has the same flaw for the same reason.

The two dominant answers split along familiar lines. Torsen centre diffs — the Audi quattro approach on longitudinal-engine cars — are purely mechanical, always active, and typically bias rearward (around 40:60) with the ability to shift much further toward whichever axle has grip. Clutch-based systems like Haldex run a multi-plate clutch, usually on a front-drive platform, sending torque rearward when sensors detect or predict slip. Lighter, cheaper, packages into transverse layouts, and historically felt more like front-wheel drive doing its best.

And here is the loveliest detail in the whole subject. The Torsen has a known weakness called the zero-torque problem: because it works by multiplying available torque, a wheel with genuinely zero traction gives it nothing to multiply, and the mechanical marvel briefly behaves like a plain open diff. Audi's fix is EDL — electronic differential lock — which applies the brake to the spinning wheel to manufacture artificial resistance so the Torsen has something to bite against.

The finest mechanical differential ever built needs the brakes to bail it out. The 1971 idea rescues the 1951 one.

We've covered this ground The larger question of front, rear and all-wheel drive — what each layout does to handling, weight, cost and weather capability — is the subject of our earlier piece, Front, Rear, or All Four. This section is deliberately brief to avoid repeating it.
VI · What You Actually Want

Choosing, and
Using It

The honest scorecard. Note that the right answer for most drivers, most of the time, is the boring one at the bottom.

TypeHow it behavesStrong atWeak atFound on
Open Always splits torque 50/50; speed goes anywhere Cornering, cost, efficiency, refinement Any unequal-grip situation The overwhelming majority of cars ever built
Clutch LSD Friction plates resist speed difference Tuneable, strong, good for track and drift use Plates wear; needs specific oil; can chatter Performance rear-drive cars, older muscle
Viscous LSD Silicone fluid thickens under shear Passive, sealed, smooth, no maintenance Slow to react; fades when hot; degrades with age 1990s AWD, older Subaru centre diffs
Torsen / helical Worm gears bias torque toward the gripping side Instant, seamless, no wear items, excellent on road Zero-grip or lifted wheel defeats it Audi quattro, Miata, Mustang, many performance cars
Auto locker Locked by default, ratchets apart in turns Massive off-road traction, no driver input Noisy, abrupt, poor on-road manners Detroit Locker and similar; serious off-road builds
Selectable locker Fully open until you press the switch Best of both — civil on road, unbeatable off it Cost, complexity, driver must remember to disengage Wrangler Rubicon, Ram Power Wagon, ARB retrofits
Brake traction control Brakes the spinning wheel to fake resistance Nearly free; works on any axle; no extra hardware Generates heat; less effective under sustained load Effectively every modern vehicle
Torque vectoring Deliberately overdrives the outer wheel in a corner Sharpens turn-in, reduces understeer Expensive in its true mechanical form Honda SH-AWD, Focus RS, modern performance AWD
Motor per wheel No shared shaft; torque is a software value Total control, instant, no mechanical limits Requires electric drive; nothing to feel or fix Multi-motor EVs

How to actually use a locker

Engage it before you need it, at low speed, ideally when stopped — not while a wheel is already spinning. Keep speeds low while it's locked. Expect the steering to push wide and the tyres to protest. Disengage the moment you're back on firm ground or pavement, because a locked axle on a dry surface is chewing tyres and stressing shafts, and in the wet it will happily send you sideways. Front lockers in particular murder your steering; lock the rear first and only add the front when the rear isn't enough.

What my mother's question deserved

For the record — for a Volvo 240, for a commuter car, for essentially anyone reading this in a place where it snows — the answer is not a locker. It is winter tyres.

Every device in this article manages torque that already exists at the contact patch. None of them create grip. A limited-slip differential redistributes the small amount of traction a summer tyre has on a cold road; a proper winter tyre simply has substantially more of it, at all four corners, in braking and cornering as well as acceleration. It is the single highest-value modification available, and it is not close.

The Silver Bullet didn't need Positraction. It needed to not be on a wet lawn.

The Bottom Line

One Tyre Spins.
The Other Does Nothing.

There is something wonderful about the fact that the definitive popular explanation of the open differential was delivered by Marisa Tomei in a rented courtroom in Georgia, standing in a leopard-print outfit, being cross-examined about tyre marks.

It's wonderful partly because the film got the engineering broadly right — rare enough — but mostly because of what the scene is actually about. Vinny wins because his fiancée knows cars. The knowledge that seemed like trivia, the stuff nobody in that courtroom took seriously, turns out to be the thing that decides whether two innocent men go to prison.

A hundred and ninety-eight years ago a watchmaker solved a problem about cornering, and in solving it created a smaller problem that has occupied engineers ever since. Clutch packs. Cone clutches. Silicone fluid. Worm gears that can drive but not be driven. Ratcheting lockers that bang like a dropped toolbox. Air lines, vacuum actuators, dial-operated hubs on cold mornings. Then sensors, then software, then a brake caliper used as a lie detector, and finally an electric motor at each corner that makes the whole magnificent argument unnecessary.

All of it, every patent and every part number, to answer a question that a woman in the passenger seat of a stuck Volvo wagon worked out on her own in about four seconds.

How do they make a car with one-wheel drive?

Because the alternative was a car that couldn't turn a corner. Everything since has been us trying to have both.

Carveyance
Sources: Britannica and Wikipedia on Onésiphore Pecqueur and differential history, including the disputed Antikythera and south-pointing chariot attributions; Gleason Works material and US Patent 2,559,916 on the Torsen principle; HandWiki, contemporary GM press and enthusiast archives on Buick MaxTrac (1971) and the Cadillac Traction Monitoring System (1979); manufacturer documentation on Haldex, quattro and EDL; My Cousin Vinny (1992, Twentieth Century Fox), dialogue quoted from published transcripts for commentary. Torque bias figures are indicative ranges and vary by design and condition. Nothing here is a substitute for your vehicle's owner's manual — locker and terrain-mode procedures differ meaningfully between models.
